name: Fats Navarro
abstract: Theodore "Fats" Navarro (September 24, 1923 – July 7, 1950) was an American jazz trumpet player and a pioneer of the bebop style of jazz improvisation in the 1940s. A native of Key West, Florida, he toured with big bands before achieving fame as a bebop trumpeter in New York.

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: I didn't realize that the solo had any significance until I met Fats Navarro, the great trumpet player, in 1948.

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: Basically, it was Miles and Dizzy and Fats Navarro. ... Those are the ones that really influenced me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Don Menza and Sam Noto
about_quotes.evidence: And then of course then Miles and Clifford and Fats Navarro was a big influence.
about_quotes.doc_title: Don Menza and Sam Noto interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, January 28, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Jason Palmer
about_quotes.evidence: I transcribed as many different trumpet players as a could... going to the library and listening to LPs of all the players that influenced the players that I had already listened to, you know Fats Navarro
about_quotes.doc_title: Jason Palmer interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, May 2, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: And in the trumpet section he had Dizzy, Fats Navarro, Shorty McConnell... Tommy Potter playing bass. Well, Tommy got a case of pneumonia, so they called me out to substitute for him

about_quotes.speaker: JJ Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: Fats Navarro and I were road buddies. We rode side by side. We’d sit side by side on the bus. We roomed together. We were true buddy- buddy buddies.
about_quotes.doc_title: JJ Johnson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
